title: 1.3.5 The collections module
summary: Purpose-built containers that kill the .get/.setdefault ceremony. defaultdict(factory) auto-creates a default on a missing key (int→0 for counting, list→[] for grouping). Counter(iterable) counts in one call (missing→0, .most_common). deque = O(1)-both-ends queue (recap from 1.5). All three are just dict/list with extra behavior.
sessionNotes: Mini-lesson, syntax-first (concept already known). Directly retires the recurring .get-vs-setdefault confusion (Q6, S10) by showing defaultdict as 'setdefault automated'. group_anagrams left as a genuine rep — the problem the user referenced. Capture answers + critiques after attempts.
intro: You already know counting and grouping — and you just nailed why .get differs from .setdefault. collections gives you containers that do both with NO ceremony: defaultdict (auto-creates a default on a missing key), Counter (counts an iterable in one call), and deque (the O(1)-both-ends queue from 1.5). All three ARE dicts/lists with extra behavior — no new concepts, just better tools.

body.heading: Refresher — the manual way (you know this; open if you want)

blocks.content:
Counting:  d[k] = d.get(k, 0) + 1   (default 0, you reassign)
Grouping:  d.setdefault(k, []).append(v)   (default [], append in place)

collections automates both so you stop writing these by hand.

body.heading: defaultdict — auto-creates a default on a missing key
body.content:
defaultdict(factory): the first time you touch a MISSING key, it calls factory() to build a default, stores it, and returns it — so d[k] 'just works', no .get / .setdefault.

• defaultdict(int)  → missing key becomes int()  = 0  → counting:  d[k] += 1
• defaultdict(list) → missing key becomes list() = [] → grouping:  d[k].append(v)

It is literally setdefault, automated. Gotcha: merely ACCESSING a missing key creates it (so a stray d[k] read inserts an entry).

body.heading: defaultdict in action
Counter — counting, batteries included
body.content:
Counter(iterable) counts everything in ONE call and returns a dict-like result.

• Missing keys return 0 (never KeyError).
• .most_common(n) → the top n as (item, count) pairs.
• Supports +, -, &, | between two Counters.

It's a dict subclass, so it behaves like a dict everywhere a dict is expected.

body.heading: Counter in action
body.content:
from collections import Counter

c = Counter('leetcode')
print(c)                   # Counter({'e': 3, 'l': 1, 't': 1, 'c': 1, 'o': 1, 'd': 1})
print(c['e'], c['z'])      # 3 0   (missing key -> 0, no KeyError)
print(c.most_common(2))    # [('e', 3), ('l', 1)]

body.heading: deque — recap from 1.5
body.content:
from collections import deque

q = deque([1, 2, 3])
q.append(4)        # add right
q.appendleft(0)    # add left
q.popleft()        # 0   (remove left, O(1))
q.pop()            # 4   (remove right, O(1))
# Double-ended, O(1) both ends. Use for queues / BFS. (You built a Queue on it in 1.5.)

body.heading: Which to reach for
body.content:
counting     -> Counter(iterable)   OR  defaultdict(int)  then  d[k] += 1
  grouping     -> defaultdict(list)                          then  d[k].append(v)
  queue / BFS  -> deque                                      then  append / popleft

  All three are just dict / list with extra behavior baked in.

body.heading: E1 worked example — char_counts (study, then CLOSE and reproduce)

blocks.content: Study it, collapse this, then write E1 from memory.

from collections import Counter

def char_counts(s):
    return Counter(s)

# defaultdict version (also fine):
# def char_counts(s):
#     counts = defaultdict(int)
#     for ch in s:
#         counts[ch] += 1
#     return counts

body.heading: E2 worked example — group_by_length (study, close, reproduce)

blocks.content: Same drill. Note: no .setdefault — defaultdict(list) makes the empty list for you.

body.heading: E3 is a real rep — no worked solution
body.content: group_anagrams is genuine problem-solving (the 'abc/cab/bac' problem you mentioned). The trick: anagrams share the SAME letters, so their SORTED letters are identical — use ''.join(sorted(word)) as a defaultdict(list) key, and words with the same signature land in the same bucket. Return the buckets with list(groups.values()). The collections part is easy; the insight (sorted-letters key) is the rep.

testSetup: from collections import Counter, defaultdict, deque

exercises.title: E1 — char_counts (Counter)
exercises.prompt: Return a count of each character in string s (a dict mapping char → count). Use Counter (one call) or defaultdict(int). Study the worked example, collapse it, then write from memory.

exercises.title: E2 — group_by_length (defaultdict)
exercises.prompt: Return a dict mapping each word LENGTH → the list of words of that length (keep input order). Use defaultdict(list) — no .setdefault. Study the worked example, close it, reproduce.

exercises.title: E3 — group_anagrams (real rep)
exercises.prompt: Group words that are anagrams of each other; return a list of groups (each a list of the words). Order within/among groups doesn't matter (tests normalize). Hint: anagrams share the same SORTED letters → use ''.join(sorted(word)) as a defaultdict(list) key, then return list(groups.values()). No worked solution on purpose.
tests: assert sorted(sorted(g) for g in group_anagrams(['eat','tea','tan','ate','nat','bat'])) == sorted(sorted(g) for g in [['eat','tea','ate'],['tan','nat'],['bat']])
assert sorted(sorted(g) for g in group_anagrams(['abc'])) == [['abc']]

reviewItemsAdded: defaultdict(factory): a missing key auto-creates factory() (int→0, list→[]); 'setdefault automated'
Counter(iterable) counts in one call; missing key → 0; .most_common(n)
group_anagrams: ''.join(sorted(word)) as the shared bucket key
